title: What do I do if my patient is allergic to hyaluronidase (Hyalase)？ | Dr Tim's Tips
filename: What do I do if my patient is allergic to hyaluronidase (Hyalase)？ ｜ Dr Tim_s Tips.en.vtt
excerpt: What do i do if a client is allergic to highlays so really common question uh hyaluronidase has an allergy rate in the data of about one in two thousand [Music] i suspect it'll be higher than that because um if you actually do allergy tests for your elective reversals you'll find…

segments.text: what do i do if a client is allergic to highlays so really common question uh hyaluronidase has an allergy rate in the data of about one in two thousand [Music] i suspect it'll be higher than that because um if you actually do allergy tests for your elective reversals you'll find it's more than one in two thousand you have not just a little bit of redness a little bit of erythema but a distinctive uh reaction i've certainly seen dozens over the ten years i've been injecting there it is possible that you'll be allergic to it um hyaluronidase is a protein so people are more likely to be to react to protein it's also not a protein that made by the human body it's actually normally unfortunately harvested from the testicles of goats so you have to think that your body is likely to produce an antibody to that certainly if you're repeatedly exposed to it you will have people who are allergic and then the question is what do we do those people so the first thing it depends on the circumstance you can we have been using non-reversible products for decades so if your patient is happy

with that risk and also importantly if you are happy with that risk then you could do the treatment knowing that you're not going to be able to reverse it and if it doesn't work out for them they'll have to wait for it to dissolve naturally or that they risk complications that can't be treated such as a you know even a vascular occlusion that's of course the thing we worry about the most so the question is um around the two risk tolerance firstly you need to as with all medical procedures we need to justify the risk according to the well-being of the patient so you would i would take that into account and i have in the past done this with someone who has a really significant issue it's easier if you know that you can remove that issue with a dermal filler treatment it's easier to justify the risk because you can say this person is going to get a huge boost in well-being but similarly on the other end of the spectrum you might argue you know this is only a tiny little thing that you're not that bothered by why don't you not do this treatment and not take the risk because if something goes wrong i can't treat you so you're always weighing up that risk between and the risk and the benefits so what are you doing for patient and what is the risk to them and making sure that they own that decision because it's not

just okay that you own it they need to own it and of course the other side of it is you're the one who's taking responsibility for this treatment so i always say to clinicians you need to own the risk yourself are you okay taking this risk because you're the one who's going to get the call and have to deal with it if there's a problem and it is absolutely acceptable for everyone else in town to doing a treatment that you think is not worth the risk for you for your well-being for the amount of time you spend having to think and worry about it so there is a decision to make for you about whether you would like to treat patients who you cannot use the reversing agent on and to think carefully about that and then stick by it
